content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 33 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 118r–119v (LC PDF pp. 234–235) | **Source Images:** DEU_033_page_001.jpg, DEU_033_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 165–166 (pa_0165–pa_0166)

*Note: Chapter 33 contains both a poetic frame (vv.1–5, 26–29) and individual tribal blessings (vv.6–25). The Leningrad Codex sets the opening and closing sections in a distinctive poetic layout, visible in the manuscript images. The tribal blessings are written in running prose with verse divisions marked.*

---

**1** And this is the blessing [berakhah] with which Moshe the man of God blessed the sons of Yisra'el before his death. **2** And he said:

The LORD came from Sinai                        and he dawned for them from Se'ir;
he shone forth from Mount Paran,               and he came from Merivat-Kadesh
[with] myriads of holy ones,                       from his right hand — a fiery law for them.
**3**
Indeed, a lover of peoples —                     all his holy ones are in your hand,
and they bowed down at your feet,             they lift up [the burden of] your words.
**4**
The instruction [torah] which Moshe commanded us — an inheritance of the congregation of Ya'akov.
**5**
And he became king in Yeshurun               when the heads of the people were gathered,
together the tribes of Yisra'el.

**6** Let Re'uven live and not die, and let his men be [though] few in number.

**7** And this for Yehudah; and he said:
Hear, O LORD, the voice of Yehudah,         and to his people bring him;
his hands will contend for him                    and a helper against his adversaries you will be.

**8** And of Levi he said:
Your Tummim and your Urim belong to your devout man [chasid],
whom you tested at Massah                       and with whom you contended at the waters of Merivah;
**9**
who said of his father and his mother: "I have not seen them,"
and his brothers he did not acknowledge      and his sons he did not know —
for they kept your word                              and your covenant they guarded.
**10**
They shall teach your ordinances to Ya'akov   and your instruction [torah] to Yisra'el;
they shall place incense in your nostrils        and a whole burnt offering upon your altar.
**11**
Bless, O LORD, his strength,                      and the work of his hands accept;
strike through the loins of those who rise against him   and of those who hate him, that they rise no more.

**12** Of Binyamin he said:
The beloved of the LORD shall dwell in security beside him;
he shields over him all the day,                  and between his shoulders he dwells.

**13** And of Yosef he said:
Blessed by the LORD is his land —             from the precious things of the heavens, from the dew,
and from the deep crouching beneath,
**14**
and from the precious produce of the sun     and from the precious growth of the months,
**15**
and from the top of the ancient mountains    and from the precious things of the eternal hills,
**16**
and from the precious things of the earth and its fullness,
and the good-will of the one who dwelt in the bush —
let it come upon the head of Yosef,            upon the crown of the one set-apart from his brothers.
**17**
His firstborn bull — majesty is his,              and his horns are the horns of the wild ox [re'em];
with them he shall gore the peoples together, the ends of the earth;
and they are the myriads of Efrayim            and they are the thousands of Menasheh.

**18** And of Zevulun he said:
Rejoice, Zevulun, in your going out,            and Yissakhar in your tents.
**19**
Peoples they will call to the mountain;          there they will offer righteous sacrifices;
for the abundance of the seas they will suck  and the hidden treasures of the sand.

**20** And of Gad he said:
Blessed is he who makes Gad large;           as a lioness he dwells and tears [at] the arm — even the crown.
**21**
And he saw the first part for himself,            for there the plotter's portion was concealed;
and he came [with] the heads of the people;  the righteousness of the LORD he did,
and his ordinances with Yisra'el.

**22** And of Dan he said:
Dan is a lion's cub                                    that leaps from Bashan.

**23** And of Naftali he said:
Naftali, satisfied with favor                         and full of the blessing of the LORD —
the west and the south possess.

**24** And of Asher he said:
Most blessed of sons is Asher;                   let him be pleasing to his brothers
and let him dip his foot in oil.
**25**
Iron and bronze are your bars,                   and as your days, so your strength.

**26** There is none like God, O Yeshurun,     riding the heavens in your help,
and in his majesty the clouds.
**27**
A refuge is the God of ancient [days],          and beneath are the everlasting arms;
and he drives out the enemy from before you  and says: "Destroy!"
**28**
And Yisra'el dwells in safety,                      alone — the spring of Ya'akov —
upon a land of grain and new wine;             even his heavens drip dew.
**29**
How blessed are you, O Yisra'el!               Who is like you, a people saved by the LORD?
The shield of your help                               and the sword of your majesty —
and your enemies will cringe before you,       and you will tread upon their high places.

[Ashreikhah Yisra'el — How blessed are you, O Yisra'el!]

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**V.2 — "fiery law":** אֵשׁ דָּת (esh dat), found only here in the entire MT. The noun dat is a hapax in this form and is possibly Aramaic/Persian in origin (cognate to the Aramaic דָּת meaning "decree/law"). The LXX Vaticanus reads ἐκ δεξιῶν αὐτοῦ ἄγγελοι μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ ("from his right hand, angels with him"), which may reflect a Vorlage reading אֵשׁ מֵאַשְׁדָּת or the translator's interpretive expansion. EMET follows the MT "fiery law" as the lectio difficilior.

**V.2 — "Merivat-Kadesh":** The LC reads מֵרִבְבֹת קֹדֶשׁ (me-rivvot qodesh, "from myriads of holiness/holy ones"), not "Merivat-Kadesh." EMET follows the consonantal text with the most natural reading: the LORD comes with "myriads of holy ones" (a heavenly retinue). The phrase "Merivat-Kadesh" appears in v.8 in connection with Levi's testing. The bracket in v.2 notes the ambiguity.

**V.3 — "a lover of peoples":** אַף חֹבֵב עַמִּים (af chovev ammim) — an extraordinarily compressed phrase. The LXX reads καὶ ἐφείσατο τοῦ λαοῦ αὐτοῦ ("and he spared his people"), reflecting a different reading or free rendering. "Indeed, a lover of peoples" preserves the MT's tenderness.

**V.5 — "he became king":** וַיְהִי בִישֻׁרוּן מֶלֶךְ (vayhi vi-Yeshurun melekh). The referent is ambiguous — is the LORD or Moshe the one who "became king in Yeshurun"? The LXX reading and the contextual flow support the LORD as king. EMET translates accordingly, with the LXX confirming: καὶ ἔσται ἐν τῷ ἠγαπημένῳ ἄρχων.

**V.8 — "your devout man":** לְאִישׁ חֲסִידֶךָ (le-ish chasidekha) — "to the man of your covenant-faithfulness." This refers to Levi, though some read it as Moshe himself (cf. Ps 106:16 where Aharon is called "holy one"). The LXX renders τῷ ἀνδρὶ τῷ ὁσίῳ ("to the holy man"), slightly less personal.

**V.9 — "I have not seen them":** לֹא רְאִיתִיו (lo re'itivu) — singular suffix in MT ("I did not see him"), perhaps referring specifically to the father. Qere/Kethiv discussion is attested in some manuscripts. The LXX smooths to plural.

**V.12 — "he shields over him":** חֹפֵף עָלָיו (chofef alav) — a rare verb meaning to cover/overshadow. The LXX renders σκεπάσει ("will cover/shelter"), confirming the LC.

**V.16 — "the one who dwelt in the bush":** שֹׁכְנִי סְנֶה (shokhni seneh) — a direct allusion to the burning bush theophany of Exodus 3. The LXX renders τοῦ κατασκηνώσαντος ἐν τῇ βάτῳ, confirming the LC exactly.

**V.17 — "wild ox":** רְאֵם (re'em) — the great aurochs (Bos primigenius), now extinct. Not "unicorn" (KJV error) but a real horned bovine of enormous power. The LXX renders μονοκέρωτος ("unicorn/one-horned"), which is the LXX's standard rendering of re'em throughout.

**V.21 — "the plotter's portion":** חֶלְקַת מְחֹקֵק (chelqat mechoqeq) — "the portion of the lawgiver/commander." Refers to Moshe's burial site, which tradition associates with the territory of Gad (near Mount Nevo). The verse is among the most obscure in the blessing.

**V.25 — "as your days, so your strength":** כְּיָמֶיךָ דָּבְאֶךָ (ke-yamekha dav'ekha) — the last word is a hapax. "Strength" follows most ancient versions including the LXX (ἡ ἰσχύς). Some propose "your waning/diminishment," which would be an ironic reversal of blessing. EMET follows the LXX and majority tradition: "strength."

**V.29 — ashreikhah Yisra'el:** "How blessed are you, O Yisra'el!" The climactic exclamation. The LXX pa_0166 renders μακάριος εἶ, Ισραηλ ("blessed are you, Israel"), a close parallel. The word ashreikhah is the same root as Psalm 1:1 ("blessed is the man"). EMET preserves the exclamatory force.

**Tribal order:** The LC order is: Reuben, Judah, Levi, Benjamin, Joseph (Ephraim/Manasseh), Zebulun/Issachar, Gad, Dan, Naphtali, Asher. Simeon is notably absent — the only tribe unblessed, understood as a consequence of Gen 49:5–7. The LXX follows the same order and omission.

— *EMET*
